item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have translated it into italian.
I am quite disappointed: the tests could have been written in a more orderly and organized way. Alas. In any case, thanks to the author for writing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't super good, but it's still leagues ahead 005 in terms of concept. "lock what opens doors" can be done in many more ways then one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
005 copy kind of, I thought it was pretty crap honestly

- 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if you use it unlock a door heading outside?
I can see two possibilities.

Either an XK event, with all mankind not inside at the time murdered to death.

Or it affects everyone inside the building same as usual, just including the key-turner this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yikes okay, this one gives me complete chills. Makes me sad that something as simple as accidentally using the wrong key can lead to a horrible fate for many.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am totally necroposting, but I guess I would like to still commend you on your creativity in expanding this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that you could go in some pretty interesting directions with this. Would the key work on animals, and not just humans? Would the level of sentience of the subject matter? What if D-class personnel with extensive self defense or martial arts training were put in the room? You could also play with dimensions of the room and door. All in all, it's a good SCP, and I think it could be made even better.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article was created by mercio and is used here with their permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Overpowered" was, perhaps an inelegant way of expressing my concerns. I of course don't mean that the extent of an SCP's abilities is an indicator of its worthiness as an SCP (else, what could be said about 682? An SCP I think works very well).
However, giving an SCP powers or abilities excessive to its purpose can detract from the narrative. SCP-682 works because it is an amusing thought experiment to come up with methods to try and kill it, followed by ways to thwart those methods. SCP-1016 works because it plays upon the common, latent fear that one may be inadvertently responsible for the death of a loved one. Affecting an area the size of an apartment or private home is sufficient to that purpose.
If the key is capable of wiping out everyone in a convention center with the flick of a wrist, it ceases to be an innocuous mistake that leads to personal tragedy (something we all fear) and becomes a weapon of mass destruction.
Scary, maybe, but no more so than an atom bomb or machine gun.

As for the crosstesting, I would hardly call it "gratuitous." The Foundation is a scientific institution; are you telling me that the possible interaction between SCP-1016 and SCP-184 are not scientifically relevant? That the crosstest would not yield a greater understanding of one or both objects?
I don't think my suggestion is out of line with the writing guidelines for "gratuitous cross linking" either, as it doesn't affect this SCP as a stand-alone article, nor act as a "vote-leech" piggybacking on SCP-182's success. Neither SCP is directly related and both function without the other. To me, the link is sensible in the context of reasonable behavior for an inquisitive organization in posession of both objects.
If the author doesn't feel like crosstesting them, I have absolutely no objection whatsoever. I do object to the suggestion that to crosstest them (if the author were so inclined) would be gratuitous or unjustified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whether something is "overpowered" or not has no bearing on how well it does as a SCP article. We're a creative writing website, not a roleplay or video game resource, so I don't see what that has to do with anything.

Also, gratuitous cross-testing or cross-linking is generally discourged, so recommending that is a pretty poor idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just how far does the key's definition of "structure" extend? Could a malicious individual wipe out an entire Foundation facility simply by locking and unlocking one entrance? What about a Subway system, shopping mall or other vast-but-contiguous enclosed space?
It seems like these are questions to which it is in the Foundation's best interest to answer, considering its potentialities as a weapon of murder on a massive scale.

If the key does effect all individuals inside a contiguous, enclosed structure, regardless of the interior volume of said space, it comes across as a tad OP. Maybe the key should become progressively more difficult to turn in proportion to the number of victims it must affect, to the point where it is beyond human strength to turn.
Either that or more explicitly define the scope of its carnage.

Might also be worth crosstesting with The Architect

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a lot here that's pretty unnecessary, and there's also a lot of potential in this. I'd love to see a test log in which several attempts are made to manually observe what goes on, or have the people inside defend themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Obviously a time stop is happening here. The world is stopped, and the people beyond the door are live, plus an intruder.

Need to mention an experiment log with armed D-class personnel, if just for the sake of saying armed victims don't trigger the effect. You could present a "bait" person in the room, plus an incredibly well-defended "observer" in the room. The observer could be surrounded by a clear plastic bulletproof case immersed in acid.

And when the observer is STILL killed, with no marks inside the bulletproof case but the victim's own frantic attempts to escape…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder what happens if there's only one door and no windows. Is the thing that did it still there breathing? If there's no way out, does it have to go through the person opening the door?

A good test for class D's, and a reasonable candidate for redaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little tardy to the party, but I have a fifth scenario.

-Everything is normal and unremarkable until key is inserted. When key is inserted, lights dim but everything else is observable and normal. Once the unlocked door clears the frame, the room is suddenly, and without transistion, a crime scene. In the recordings, there is simply a jump, like bad editing, but of course no evidence of editing or tampering with the footage can be found.

My thinking is that the effect only occurs at that particular instant, so there is nothing untoward to observe externaly until that instant. Either the effect just transmutes the interior into evidence that seems several hours old, or the effect is retro-active in time but constrained to the unlocked area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could be used as an assassination tool. Slip it on the keyring of someone who works for the person you want dead (housekeeper, etc), wait a whille and, voila, one (or more) dead people. Just don't be in the room with them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except that it only activates when unlocking. So it's more like, guy comes home from work, unlocks the door, and when he goes in his family is dead.

That's exactly why I like this.

I agree on the "why would you put this on your keychain in the first place?" thing, though.

I dunno, I put keys on my keychain for no good reason sometimes because I think that I've forgotten what that particular key went to. And I'd assume that the bloodstains were paint or obstinate gunk or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, very much liking these considerations, wish someone had brought this up before I posted. I'm most liking option 3. Since the primal fear I'm trying to play on is "home invasion" I'm probably going to expand on the "someone's tampering" aspect by having curtains drawn, furniture moved in front of windows, that sort of thing. Just need time to sit down and focus on it, should have it up in a day or two.

I really appreciate the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very good suggestions, KeterSatyr, listen to them.

I particularly prefer the third option. Regarding the point eric_h raised, windows in the house could become foggy, frosted, or shatter partially, so that glass would remain in place but completely covered in cracks so nothing is visible through them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You might have to go with 2. or else you could make a glass house, put some D-Class in it, and have someone unlock it, while others observe (and then throw stones).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Experiments involving autonomously-powered video surveillance of the interior show the video monitoring devices to malfunction as power is restored, in a manner consistent with manual tampering; batteries are removed, lenses covered, or power is turned off.

First, a word-choice nitpick: I think it should be "independently-powered", not "autonomously-powered".

Substantial criticism: what you've done to keep what's happening inside mysterious only works for observers that are inside the house being unlocked. For example, if the Foundation set up an experiment with a house with uncovered windows in every wall, then both recording equipment and watching humans outside the house could see what happened. The SCP could disable all of the outside recording devices, but that still leaves the problem of the observing humans. Some possibilities:

  1. Right when the door is unlocked all of the outside observers sneeze, are distracted by anything, or whatever, and don't see what happened, with all of the outside recording devices having been disabled during the sneeze.
  2. Right when the door in unlocked all of the outside observers are teleported to inside of the house and murdered, along with anyone observing the observers, anyone observing them, and so forth.
  3. Right when the door is unlocked blinds are dropped/drawn over the windows (if there weren't any blinds in the house before, there are now). None of the outside video recording devices have been disabled. However, reviews of the recording show that the blinds weren't pulled when the door was unlocked, but several hours before, with audio recordings of the observers saying things like "where the [EXPLETIVE] did those blinds come from" (voice analysis shows the voices to be an exact match for the voices for the observers). The recordings also show the observers spending several hours trying to gain access to the house, but somehow failing. The observers all swear up and down that nothing like that happened.
  4. The observers are in voice-only contact with someone who isn't at or near the house, with communications happening for several hours before the house is unlocked. Right when the door is unlocked communication is briefly interrupted. When communication is restored the observers don't remember communicating with the other person for the span of several hours, but instead insist that they all fell unconscious several hours ago and are now just waking up. Inspection of the external video recorders shows footage of the observers all falling to the ground unconscious, shortly after which the recorders are all disabled. Further, immediate medical inspection of the bruises the observers have show them all being several hours old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This needs a bit of trimming and stream-lining. I think it would help if the article was shortened considerably.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "alibi" part comes in because according to the description, forensics would insist that the whole thing took hours of violent struggle. If he could prove he had been somewhere else for all of those hours, well, there's your alibi. (There's also the fact that he would show no signs of defensive wounds, or for that matter having been in a struggle at all - really, a conviction tells us more about how bad his defense attorney was than anything else.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheesy question time: Would the dimming affect bioluminescent marine life?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except that it only activates when unlocking. So it's more like, guy comes home from work, unlocks the door, and when he goes in his family is dead.

I agree on the "why would you put this on your keychain in the first place?" thing, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All in all, it seems somewhat mashed together. Keys that magically turn into other keys, when they open doors they kill anyone inside and it might be ritualistic! Maybe! Oh and the person who opens them hears breathing so it might be a thing killing them!

I dunno, it felt coherent to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What tipped it over for me was the guy they got it from. You can just see this guy innocently locking his front door and suddenly hearing his family cry out. He rushes in to find them brutally murdered, calls the police, and gets rushed through the system into a conviction. Although, it does make me wonder why he's judged to have had such a good alibi if he were arguably right next to the house when they got murdered. Even if someone saw him on the front porch step at the right time, there's nothing which would forgo him having murdered them, stepped outside, and then rushing in.

I agree with the others that the empty windows and heavy breathing detract from it. A key which kills everyone inside is good enough as it is.

It does kind of make me wonder why anyone would put this on their keychain, bloodstains and all, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Think I misunderstood a field name. Thank you for the assistance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has been offered at several points in the chat, with criticism being taken into account and almost universally applied. The last time I showed it, there were four reactions in the nature of, "Creepy! Post it!" and no further criticism, so I figured it was ready. Did you offer me feedback that I failed to properly apply?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i like the core concept to this quite a bit, a key that mysteriously kills all the occupants of the room it is used to open. that's pretty creepy. but other parts of the article arent quite so good. the implication from the heavy breathing and such of there being an entity observed feels like it's added in to make it "scarier"(it doesnt). the bloodstains are a bit meh.

the key morphing to resemble other keys is good. video surveillance being interrupted and the way people are killed are good.

this basically reminds me of SCP-342, but in key form…but that's a good thing, because this definitely takes a different direction, and 342 is one of the best.

you're going negative here, pull it down, rework it, ask for thoughts from people in chat, and repost it, because i'd actually like to see this one survive and do well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some kinda weird phrasing issues ("when the unlocking act occurs"), but I like the idea. I like it specifically because it's like you use the key to let yourself into a room, and you're letting something out at the same time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember when this was offered in the chat and given maybe a lukewarm response. At least, I don't remember getting any 'omg yesplz'. Maybe my memory is slipping.

All in all, it seems somewhat mashed together. Keys that magically turn into other keys, when they open doors they kill anyone inside and it might be ritualistic! Maybe! Oh and the person who opens them hears breathing so it might be a thing killing them!

It all in all reminds me of an Umineko murder scene, but in a bad way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept. There is some clumsy wording in some places, you need to fix it. Not the best usage of [DATA EXPUNGED] too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the title. Titles are always SCP-XXXX, never the 'name' of the article.
